data.title: Marqeta: Slowing Growth And Block Dependency Keep Valuation In Check
data.description:
Introduction

Marqeta, Inc. (MQ) is a modern payment card issuing platform, providing cloud services-based infrastructure via APIs. Its business model is focused on transaction processing fees, which directly correlate to the total volume of payments that have passed through the platform, which reached $383 billion in 2025.

The main company‘s problem remains revenue concentration risk, because Block, Inc generates around 44% of all the company‘s revenues, which creates constant pressure in negotiations on service rates. Even though yearly revenues in 2025 grew 23%, reaching $625 million, gross margin stabilized at 70%.

Currently, the company‘s P/S ratio is at 2.8, which reflects the market‘s reorientation from earlier high growth expectations to a more realistic profitability valuation. The company in 2025 recorded a $14 million GAAP net loss, though the management is forecasting that in 2026, there will be a breaking point and it will reach around $10 million in net profits. Such a financial structure shows that economies of scale are starting to offset operating costs, though in 2026, the growth forecast is moderate at around 12-14% due to Block's transition to lower pricing and diversification of Cash App card issuance. Investors value the company as a critical part in fintech infrastructure, though the decreasing take rate ratio and the dependency on one massive client remain the main factors that are limiting share valuation multiple expansion.

Business overview

Marqeta operates as a modern card issuer and payment processing infrastructure provider, its business model is based on commission fees for the volume of transactions processed. In 2025, the company reached a record $383 billion yearly operational value, which marked a 31% growth compared to 2024.

Despite the reached volumes, in 2026, the company is forecasting a moderate revenue growth for this fiscal year. The main thing that is slowing the company down is the Block's change in contract pricing level and two other large client contract renewals, which in 2026 will artificially reduce gross profit growth by around 7%.

Trying to soften this change, Marqeta is managing a purposeful product rotation, increasing higher margin services and loan solutions, especially the weight of buy now pay later segments in the portfolio. At the end of 2025, value-added services were already generating 7% of the gross profit, and loan service volumes grew almost 60% in a year. This change is necessary to maintain a higher revenue collection rate.

Even though the market‘s valuation remains conservative with a 2.8 price to sales ratio, 2026 should be the breaking point where it‘s going to reach a positive net income by the general accounting principles, estimated at $10 million. Diversification in Europe, where volumes are growing two times faster than the overall portfolio, helps consistently lower the dependency on Block, whose concentration in revenues decreased 44%.

Valuation

Marqeta‘s valuation as of today reflects the market‘s skepticism due to the slowing growth and a high revenue concentration risk. The company‘s price-to-sales ratio is currently reaching 2.8, which is lower than the five-year historical average of around 7x, suggesting that investors no longer credit the company's status as a high-growth technology company.

Even though the revenues in 2025 grew 23% to $625 million, the 2026 official forecast is showing a drop to 13% growth, because large clients, especially Block contract renewals, are decreasing the net margin and the potential for generating revenues. Compared to the sector‘s leaders, Marqeta is valued way lower than Adyen N.V. (ADYEY), whose P/S ratio is reaching around 11, due to the high EBITDA margin, while Marqeta in 2025 only fixed a 18% adjusted EBITDA margin.

Competitive pressure from Stripe Issuing and SOFI forces the company to reduce service prices, therefore, the balance with $1.2 billion cash, which makes up around 35% of the market‘s capitalization, gives financial stability, though the 2.2 P/B ratio shows that the market isn‘t confident in a rapid achievement of GAAP profitability. Unlike diversified competitors such as DLocal Limited (DLO) or Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM), Marqeta remains vulnerable due to only one client‘s concentration, which is why the currently the low valuation is a direct outcome of structural risk and the diminishing returns to scale.

Risks

Marqeta‘s business risks in 2026 are focused on a high concentration of income and systematic margin pressure, arising from the contract renewal cycles. The main risks remain the dependency on Block, which at the end of 2025 still generated around 48% of the company‘s revenues.

Even though the contract was extended, new conditions in 2026‘s financial forecast are expected to have a negative impact on gross profit growth due to the mandatory commission cut, while trying to maintain the largest client. Second risk for the company is the aggressive competition with Stripe and Adyen, which are offering vertically integrated payment acceptance and card issuance solutions, thereby decreasing clients need to use a separate Marqeta platform.

This is directly reflected in the net revenue retention, which dropped to 111% compared to 126% in 2024. The company also faces regulatory risks due to the US Federal Reserve Bank‘s proposed Regulation II changes, which could limit debit card interchange fees. Because MQ‘s revenue model is directly linked to part of these fees, any decrease in them directly cuts the net margins, which in 2026 Q1 should be showing signs of stabilization at around the 68-70% range.

Financial risk is the prolonged GAAP loss despite the $1.2 billion cash reserve. In 2025, net loss reached $188 million, and 2026 is supposed to be the breaking point, though it is still uncertain due to high share-based payment costs, which make up around 25% of revenues. Finally, macroeconomic sensitivity to spending in the BNPL sector, which is generating a significant part of TPV, raises the risk to transactional volumes if inflation and interest rates continue to limit middle-class consumption.

Thesis summary

In my view, Marqeta is currently in a transitional phase from being a high-growth story to becoming a more mature, profitability-focused business model. The market clearly overvalued expectations, which is seen in the contraction of the P/S ratio, which no longer reflects a high growth narrative, but rather a more conservative fintech infrastructure‘s valuation. However, this doesn‘t mean that the company lost its strategic value. On the contrary, the company remains a critical link in the modern payments ecosystem, especially in the card issuing segment.

The main question here is not growth, but structure. A high dependency on Block remains the main problem, which limits margin expansion as well as valuation multiple. Even if revenues are growing, the decreasing take rate is showing that the company doesn‘t have full bargaining power, which in the long-term pressures profitability. In my opinion, exactly this aspect explains why the market doesn‘t give the company a higher valuation even while seeing growing TPVs.

On the other hand, I see a clear strategic move in value-added services, and the BNPL segment‘s growth shows an attempt to move away from the commoditized processing model. If this transformation works out, Marqeta could gradually increase margins and lower dependency on one client. Though this is not a quick process, and 2026 will most likely be a proof-of-concept, rather than a full breaking point.

In conclusion, I value Marqeta as an infrastructure play with a clear upside if diversification happens, though the current risk structure, especially client concentration and pricing pressure, limits a bullish valuation.

data.title: The IPO Window Reopens For Small Caps
data.description:
U.S. initial public offerings surged 40 percent in 2025 to 371 listings, with a pipeline of high-profile candidates positioning 2026 as a potential landmark year for the small- and mid-cap ecosystem.

A Market Coming Back to Life

The U.S. IPO market produced 371 listings in 2025, a 40 percent increase from the 266 offerings in 2024, according to MicroVentures. The acceleration marks the third consecutive year of recovery from the post-2021 drought that froze new issuance across much of the venture-backed economy. The pipeline heading into 2026 includes several of the largest private companies in the world, led by Anthropic, OpenAI, SpaceX, and Canva.1

For small-cap investors, the revival of the IPO market matters beyond headline valuations. A functioning primary market brings fresh capital, institutional attention, and liquidity to the asset class. When companies like Kraken (valued at $20 billion) and Dataiku ($3.7 billion) enter the public markets, they expand the investable universe and draw analyst coverage to the smaller end of the capitalization spectrum.2

Private equity firms have been a key driver of the thaw. Firms have been holding investments longer than optimal for their fund cycles, creating what William Fahey of a major insurance provider described as “substantial pent-up demand” for exits. The absence of timely distributions has pressured limited partners. If market conditions remain stable, the exit backlog could fuel sustained issuance through 2026 and into 2027.

U.S. IPO activity has recovered sharply, with 371 listings in 2025, up 40% from 266 in 2024. Source: MicroVentures

Small Caps Lead on Valuation and Earnings

The Russell 2000 gained approximately 12 percent in the first quarter of 2026, dramatically outperforming the S&P 500’s modest 1.5 percent gain. The performance gap reflects a broader rotation away from mega-cap technology and into domestic-oriented companies. Trading volumes in small-cap indices have hit three-year highs as institutional investors rebalance portfolios.3

The valuation case remains compelling. The Russell 2000 trades at approximately 18 times forward earnings versus 26 times for the S&P 500, a 31 percent discount that represents the widest gap in over 25 years. Before the pandemic, small caps typically traded at a premium to large caps. That premium vanished during the mega-cap technology run and has yet to return.4

Earnings projections reinforce the rotation thesis. Bloomberg consensus estimates project Russell 2000 earnings growth of 43 percent over the coming twelve months, compared to 11 percent for the S&P 500. The earnings inflection, following negative growth in 2023 and 2024, has drawn fundamental investors back to the asset class. For IPO candidates evaluating timing, a strong small-cap market raises potential valuations and improves the probability of a successful offering.

The forward P/E discount of small caps vs. large caps has reached its widest level in over 25 years. Source: FactSet

Tailwinds from Policy and Dealmaking

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, passed in mid-2025, provided meaningful fiscal support for small-cap companies. The restoration of immediate research and development expensing and 100 percent bonus depreciation improved cash flow for capital-intensive manufacturers. The shift of the interest deduction limit from 30 percent of EBIT to 30 percent of EBITDA reduced the tax burden for leveraged smaller companies.5

Merger and acquisition activity is contributing additional momentum. EY-Parthenon projects a 3 percent increase in overall M&A deal volume in 2026, while deal value climbed 36 percent in the first nine months of 2025. Deloitte’s survey of dealmakers indicates that the “vast majority” expect more and bigger deals in 2026. For small caps, M&A activity can drive re-rating in stock prices and create acquisition premiums across entire sectors.6

The combination of a recovering IPO market, historically wide valuation discounts, accelerating earnings growth, and policy tailwinds creates a favorable environment for small-cap investing. The risks remain real: floating-rate debt exposure, the maturity wall, and geopolitical uncertainty all bear watching. The opportunity, however, is increasingly difficult for institutional allocators to ignore. Capital is flowing back to the smallest segment of the public equity market.

Russell 2000 earnings growth is projected at 43% for the next 12 months, compared to 11% for the S&P 500. Source: Bloomberg

Footnotes

1. MicroVentures, 2026 IPO Outlook, December 2025.

2. U.S. News, 8 Best Upcoming IPOs in 2026, updated April 2026.

3. S&P Dow Jones Indices, Russell 2000 factsheet, March 2026.

4. FactSet Earnings Insight, Forward P/E Estimates, March 2026.

5. Chronicle Journal, The Great Rotation: Russell 2000 Outshines Mega-Caps, March 24, 2026.

6. EY-Parthenon, Deal Barometer, January 2026.

data.title: Sibanye Stillwater: The South Africa Discount
data.description:
Sibanye Stillwater Limited (SBSW) is a major PGM and gold miner based in South Africa. On multiple valuation metrics, SBSW sounds cheap. However, SBSW is being discounted for a reason. More than 80% of its operations are concentrated in one geography—South Africa. Jurisdictional risks associated with this particular geography overwhelmingly drive SBSW's business and bottom line. While management is actively diversifying, it will take time, and even then, it may not be possible to divest more than 50% of their business from South Africa. That indicates that the discounted valuation is less of an opportunity and more of a risk assessment. Therefore, I rate SBSW a Sell/Avoid and may change my rating if there is meaningful geographical diversification.

Business and Geography

Sibanye Stillwater Limited is a diversified miner with the most exposure to platinum group metals (PGMs) and gold. They have a number of deep-level underground mines as well as surface operations. They mainly operate in South Africa, with a smaller footprint in the United States and Europe.

For FY2024, SBSW generated approximately ZAR 112 billion (~$6.7 billion) in revenue. Here's a breakdown from 2024, where revenue breakdown by segment is available:

In 2024, PGM accounted for ~53% of revenue, followed by gold at ~33%. In 2025, we can derive total PGM from geographical breakdown values published, which tells us that PGM was slightly higher at ~56% of total EBITDA. If you include recycling, that goes up to ~67%. Gold remained at 33% of EBITDA. PGM and gold together account for the bulk of the revenue.

As for geographic footprint, in 2024, revenue-based calculations show 73% of operations were concentrated in South Africa alone. In 2025, EBITDA-based numbers show this is now 77%. While this may not be an exact apples-to-apples comparison, it is not wrong to say that South African operations consist of a disproportionately large part of the company. Core mining assets like PGM and gold are mostly concentrated in SA. Here are two tables:

South Africa Contribution—PGM vs. Gold

SA PGM/Gold and total PGM/Gold

The data makes this clear. South Africa accounts for three-quarters of total earnings, with no meaningful change visible between 2024 and 2025. More critically, the core business—PGM and gold—is overwhelmingly exposed to that one geography.

Within PGM, ~80% of contributions come from South Africa. There is a slight improvement in US PGM share between 2024 and 2025, from 14% to 21%. From one viewpoint, that's a ~50% increase. However, it is still marginal compared to the South African share. As for gold—another core revenue driver—gold is 100% based in South Africa. There is no international component.

This explains why any jurisdictional risk overwhelmingly reflects on SBSW's valuation. If there is a discount—there is—it is not temporary but structural.

The Discount Isn't Caused by Operational Weakness

SBSW trades at a significant discount to its peers. On forward estimates, it trades at only ~3.5-3.7x earnings, where the sector median is ~16x. That's a 78% discount. On EV/EBITDA, there's again a ~73% discount: ~2.3x forward vs. ~8.4x sector. Even on sales metrics, SBSW trades at ~0.98x forward EV/Sales vs. ~1.88x, a ~48% discount.

This is not random. This is a deep and persistent discount across the entire valuation framework. It cannot be explained by operational constraints alone.

Purely operationally, SBSW is not a broken business. The miner generated ~$2.3B in EBITDA in 2025. Core segments, like the South African PGM and gold operations, are both profitable. SA PGM generated ~$1B, while gold contributed ~$750M in EBITDA. Even international mines like US PGMs and recycling are EBITDA-positive, contributing ~$260 million and ~$240 million, respectively.

SBSW's balance sheet is also strong, with an EV of ~$11.1B and net debt/EBITDA of 0.59x. It has exposure to critical minerals like platinum, palladium, and gold. None of these are structurally impaired assets.

These numbers suggest that SBSW's deep discount does not come from weak operations or lack of profitability. Rather, it points to one structural factor that cannot be easily mitigated and one that shapes how the entire business is perceived and evaluated. This is jurisdiction.

The South Africa Discount

South Africa ranks poorly as a mining jurisdiction: In its 2025 Annual Survey of Mining Companies, The Fraser Institute ranked South Africa 57th out of 68 jurisdictions on overall investment attractiveness, with a score of 52.97. The country was listed among the 10 least attractive jurisdictions globally on the Policy Perception Index, alongside countries such as Mali, Burkina Faso, the DRC, Angola, and Ecuador. This becomes important because, as Fraser notes, ~40% of mining investment decisions are driven by policy factors, not geology.

The operational backdrop remains constrained: Another resource, the SA Mine 2025 report from PwC, is more directly critical of the operating environment in South Africa. The report says there is persistent cost pressure, policy uncertainty, and infrastructure bottlenecks in the country. Power is unreliable, and mining companies face intermittent disruptions.

For SBSW, this is not theoretical. SBSW has contracted ~765 MW of private renewable capacity. It says it is now the largest private renewable energy offtaker in South African mining. This masks the fact that SBSW has been forced to take these measures just to ensure it can become free from South Africa's power problems. The company targets to meet ~56% of its energy demand from renewables by 2028. The worry, then, is for the other 44%.

Not just power, the PwC report says that logistics is another binding constraint, with Transnet's coal export line operating at only ~62% of capacity. This limits throughput despite favorable pricing.

Illegal mining and security are measurable financial risks: Further from the PwC report, South African government estimates show that there are ~6,000 abandoned mines and ~$3.6B in losses due to illegal mining. Sibanye itself reported 1,720 security incidents and ~$3.2 million in direct losses from copper theft in 2024—as cited by PwC.

Just recently, a Sibanye-related litigation attorney was shot and killed in what authorities described as a likely targeted attack. Her death created "fears from South Africa's business community," and has "intensified scrutiny around labor tensions in South Africa's mining sector, even as Sibanye Stillwater recently secured a three-year wage agreement with major unions."

South Africa's mining base is structurally difficult: PwC also notes that South Africa's gold output has declined. It used to be the world's largest producer in the 1990s—today it stands in tenth place. This has been caused by ageing mines as well as operational challenges like the ones we just highlighted. In the context of "future metals," PwC says that the country is not being adequately explored, and licensing processes are slow and non-transparent. South Africa has limited battery-grade processing capacity. There is also a skilled labor shortage in hydrometallurgy and chemical processing.

Diversification Efforts Are Real, But Not Enough

Management is aware of this situation and is clearly trying to diversify. They are building non-South African assets in recycling, US PGMs, lithium, nickel, zinc, copper, and other "green metals." They are expanding into the US, Finland, France, Australia, and Argentina.

However, the earnings base is still overwhelmingly South African. Their 100% gold component and near-80% PGM component come from that one geography. Indeed, South Africa is still a key destination for PGM, so it does not appear that SBSW will be able to divest out of the country quickly enough. For reference, their Keliber plant in Finland is going through a staged ramp-up because of lithium market volatility. Management has also hinted at internal organic growth rather than aggressive external expansion, which highlights the concern that SBSW will remain a majorly South African operation for the foreseeable future.

This is the key point. There may be marginal diversification, but not enough to impact the discount. SBSW may be an international miner, but that is very nominal. At its core, it is a South African mining business and comes with all the associated benefits and risks.

Valuation—What Needs to Change for a Re-Rating

In terms of its geographic earnings mix, SBSW, as we have seen, is overwhelmingly South African. The market is assigning one multiple for its South African earnings and a higher one for its non-South African (mostly US) earnings.

We can make an estimate about this by looking at miners like SBSW that are overwhelmingly South Africa-focused—names like Valterra Platinum Limited (ANGPY) and Impala Holdings (IMPUY) come to mind—versus a majorly US-focused miner like Newmont (NEM).

Newmont trades at ~13.1x forward earnings and ~6.8x forward EV/EBITDA. By contrast, Impala, a South Africa/Zimbabwe-heavy PGM miner, trades at only ~7.3x forward earnings and ~4.6x forward EV/EBITDA. Valterra Platinum, another South African PGM name, trades at ~6.4x forward EV/EBITDA and ~2.6x forward EV/Sales. While these are not directly comparable because of differences in mix and so on, one can see materially higher multiples for major US-based miners.

A simple framework makes clear what needs to change for SBSW. Let us assign a ~3.0-4.0x EV/EBITDA to South African operations and ~6.0-8.0x to non-South African operations to reflect the jurisdictional risk. Then, we can get a blended valuation table:

We can now see why SBSW trades at a steep discount to the ~8-9x sector median. Even if SBSW manages to expand meaningfully outside South Africa, the implied multiple only moves towards ~5x. This means that the discount comes from where earnings are generated.

If South Africa's share of EBITDA falls below ~50%, and non-South African operations scale materially, a re-rating may occur. Until then, despite strong operations and balance sheets, valuation will likely remain capped by inherent jurisdictional risk.

Conclusion—Cheap for a Reason, Not Yet for a Rerating

Sibanye Stillwater looks cheap on earnings, EBITDA, and sales not because it is being mispriced, but because the market is discounting its jurisdictional concentration. This is not merely about perception, although perception may play a role. As I have shown, major authoritative studies have highlighted the jurisdictional risks associated with mining in South Africa. Uncertainties around power, transport, licensing, labor, and other disruptions persist in South Africa, and the market is therefore unlikely to value SBSW's earnings on par with developed-market peers.

So either one bets on a high commodity upcycle that produces such strong earnings as to overwhelm the multiple issues or on a long-term geographic shift away from South Africa and other troubling geographies. Unless at least one of these happens, SBSW will continue to trade cheap. Given my focus on lower-risk assets, I will consider SBSW a sell/avoid.

data.title: Riskfield: The Growth Story Is Even Better Today
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Riskfield Ltd. (RSKD) was a buy rating because I believed the business should see growth accelerate in the coming years given how well its platform strategy is scaling and that new products are seeing solid adoption trends. My view is even more positive now. The demand story looks cleaner, the platform strategy is contributing real revenue, and retention is improving at the same time. On top of that, agentic commerce could expand the number of problems RSKD can solve, which should support growth from here.

The growth outlook got better

The most important reason to continue staying bullish on RSKD is because the demand story now looks cleaner. As a recap, the growth story logic is still largely the same, in that RSKD is seeing solid pipeline strength and healthy renewal outcomes, and the platform should slowly contribute more to growth. We now have better proof that this logic is holding true and, in fact, has gotten stronger. You only need to look at RSKD's latest numbers to arrive at this conclusion. In the recent quarter (Q4), RSKD saw the highest quarterly amount of new business since the IPO, and that one quarter accounted for ~55% of all new business won in 2025; competitive win rates were also >75%, and lead generation at the top of the funnel went up ~50% y/y. These numbers tell me that RSKD growth momentum is not slowing down at all.

The platform strategy has also gotten more tangible, as I expected. We now have good data to better link new products and actual economics. Some of these new products, including Policy Protect, Account Secure, and Dispute Resolve, have already generated ~$10 million of annual revenue in 2025, and management is guiding for this set of products plus related non-guaranteed payment flows to reach ~$15 million to $20 million in 2026. Obviously, this is not enough to carry RSKD's entire growth story, but it is large enough to matter if the pace of growth is 50% to 100% a year. If RSKD makes $20 million revenue from these products this year, that itself is $10 million incremental revenue, or ~3% revenue growth contribution. Broadly, this also shows that RSKD is able to roll out new products and convert them into actual revenue.

Even if we look at RSKD's current customer base, the demand profile is getting better too, and we can infer this from the retention data. Both annual dollar retention [ADR] and net dollar retention [NDR] improved by 400 bps and 900 bps, respectively, and at the same time, the number of merchants using >1 product went up ~50% in FY2025. Put together, this suggests RSKD is not only winning customers, but it is also able to keep them and even expand wallet share, which makes it even more embedded in how clients operate.

Agentic commerce

The last point I would emphasize is agentic commerce. I believe the rise of agentic commerce is going to make the entire operating environment much more complex for merchants and, at the same time, expand RSKD's role in solving it. There are two types of agentic flows: (1) general-purpose LLMs that help consumers discover products and move closer to checkout and (2) merchant-native AI agents that sit inside the merchant ecosystem and manage more of the full shopping journey (including things like refunds, exchanges, etc.). As you can imagine, these really enhance the customers' experience and are something merchants would likely adopt.

However, this also creates new trust and fraud issues, and my view is signal loss is the main problem. According to management in the Q4 earnings call, when consumers transact through general-purpose LLMs, ~30% to 40% of key fraud signals can be lost. Flip it around, and it means that a merchant could see fraud rates go up by a decent amount, and that is a big deal. The idea is simple: fraud prevention gets harder when there is less visibility into the normal behavior that helps separate good users from bad ones. This is where RSKD expertise comes into play, since its response is not just about scoring the transaction. It is also helping merchants manage these lower-signal environments by segmenting order flows, spotting new fraud patterns faster, and giving merchants visibility they would not otherwise have.

I see a few ways this translates into revenue growth for RSKD. First, it can capture demand from existing merchants that are starting to test agentic channels and do not want to take higher fraud losses while they experiment. Second, since RSKD is no longer only about checkout protection, it can address a wider set of fraud and trust problems, including areas like returns, refunds, disputes, and account security.

That said, I do not think the right focus is that agentic commerce will suddenly drive a near-term jump in revenue. The framing should be that this increases the importance of RSKD's platform, and since RSKD already has the data, the identity layer, and the merchant relationships, it should be well positioned to capture demand here.

Valuation

My model still points to very attractive upside for RSKD's stock. What I have upgraded here is that growth acceleration should be faster. I now expect growth to accelerate to 10% in FY2026 (this is as guided), 15% in FY2027, and 20% in FY2028, breaking past the previous hurdle of mid-teens revenue growth. I don't think my growth assumptions are overly ambitious given the win rates, lead generation growth, and AI story. For multiples, I am still of the view that it should at least go back to 1.4x, but I remind readers that the broader cybersecurity players like Fortinet (FTNT) and Check Point Software (CHKP) trade at ~7x and 4x NTM revenue with high-single-digit to mid-teen revenue growth. Assuming a 1.4x multiple, I think the stock can easily re-rate back to ~$6.8 by the end of FY2027.

Risk

The revenue acceleration story still depends on timing. RSKD has won strong new business, but if the timing of merchant launches gets delayed, reported growth will not accelerate as fast as I expected. In addition, while the agentic commerce opportunity is interesting, it is still early. If that demand takes longer to convert into meaningful volume, the market may stay unconvinced that this newer growth driver can materially drive growth.

Conclusion

My view for RSKD is still a buy rating. I remain bullish because the core growth story has gotten better. New business momentum is strong, newer products are becoming meaningful enough to support growth, and retention trends suggest RSKD is getting more embedded with customers. I also think the agentic commerce angle matters, not because it will drive an immediate revenue jump, but because it can make RSKD's platform more relevant as fraud risks get more complex.

data.title: Redwire: A Space Infrastructure Play Trying To Cover It All, But At A Cost
data.description:
Thesis

My core bull case here for Redwire Corporation (RDW) would depend on the sheer breadth and validation of their portfolio. Their involvement for me is extremely impressive in that they span across multiple high-demand segments of the space economy, as I’ll explain. This is also pretty rare for a company of their size. You see, rather than relying on a single niche, Redwire is actually very actively embedded in next-generation areas such as spacecraft systems, defence technologies, and in-space infrastructure, and now we’ve just seen a high-value quantum-secure communications contract. Each of these would represent a structurally growing market.

I last covered Redwire post FY25 earnings, and what now stands out for me is not just exposure, but also validation. There are contracts with NASA via the Artemis program, partnerships with Lockheed Martin and Airbus, and a big role in the European Space Agency QKDSat initiative. These all tell me that Redwire is pretty well trusted on mission-critical systems in both civil and defence domains. So my case here is that this diversification should reduce reliance on any single revenue stream but also set the company up to take advantage of several long-term space growth themes.

Quantum Key Distribution Satellite

Towards the beginning of April, Redwire managed to land a contract with the European Space Agency for the Quantum Key Distribution Satellite (QKDSat). Now, I see this as a pretty big step in the right direction. One very rapidly moving area of satellite defence is communications against next-generation cyber threats, particularly those that are posed by quantum computing. You see, when it comes to quantum computing, the real threat is that it has the ability to break the mathematical foundations that most encryption relies on. We have current systems like RSA or ECC that depend on problems that are extremely hard for classical computers to solve. They might be factoring very large numbers, but a sufficiently powerful quantum computer can use algorithms such as Shor's Algorithm to solve these problems exponentially faster. So the worry comes around being able to decrypt sensitive data that was once considered secure. And what makes it even more of an actionable threat today is that communications can be intercepted today and simply stored and decrypted in the future once quantum capabilities mature.

So this is where Redwire comes in. Basically, this initiative is for the deployment of a quantum-secure satellite capable of distributing encryption keys literally using the principles of quantum mechanics. Specifically, we’re talking about quantum key distribution. Now, unlike classical encryption methods, which we typically see rely on mathematical complexity and could, in theory, be broken by some very powerful quantum computers, QKD’s aim is to ensure that any attempt to intercept/eavesdrop on a communication alters the quantum state of the transmitted data. So that would then immediately reveal the intrusion. And I see the fact that Redwire was awarded this contract as a great validation sign. Their job would be to manufacture their European-built Hammerhead spacecraft and then integrate it with a highly specialised QKD payload alongside their ADPMS-3 avionics suite. This would manage things like onboard data processing, control systems, and communication interfaces. So going forward, this sets up Redwire not just as a hardware provider but more so as a key systems integrator to enable secure, end-to-end satellite communications infrastructure. The latter here is an area in space communication that should see very high demand going forward, with few validated players.

Artemis II involvement

Another showcase of their portfolio diversification would have to be their involvement in Artemis II. Again, I see this as a very important validation of their high-end space systems capabilities, particularly in mission-critical imaging/navigation. The big thing is that it’s set to be part of its contracts with Lockheed Martin, as the prime contractor for the Orion spacecraft. Redwire is then going to be responsible for the Orion Camera System, and not just for one but for Artemis missions I through V. For Artemis II specifically, this would include a pretty sophisticated suite of 11 cameras that will be placed both internally and externally on the spacecraft. The goal here is to allow for real-time inspection, situational awareness, and mission documentation. What I found notable was that Redwire’s Optical Navigation Cameras are actually going to play a direct role in spacecraft guidance. In that they’re set to feed high-resolution imagery into machine vision algorithms that can then calculate Orion’s position/velocity relative to Earth. This is an extremely essential function for deep-space navigation, where GPS is simply unavailable. They’re also tipped to add a 4K video, 12MP imaging, and livestream capability. So they clearly want the dual technical and public engagement role on show. Now, this essentially turns the mission into a globally visible event.

If we look beyond just imaging, Redwire should also play some kind of role in spacecraft survivability and power optimisation. They have what’s called a Coarse Sun Sensor assembly, which is set to be delivered in collaboration with Airbus for the European Service Module. These are basically sensors, but they are very critical for determining the Sun’s position. This is what allows Orion’s solar arrays to orient correctly, without which they can’t maintain efficient power generation. Overall, for Redwire, their contribution here is pretty big, and these technologies aren’t just experimental; they’re flight-proven from Artemis I. Previously, we saw them successfully deliver continuous imagery and operational data. So taken together, Artemis II here should show an integration into the core infrastructure of one of NASA’s most high-profile programs. Again, this is great for validation but also gives them a lot of credibility as a supplier of precision avionics and optical systems. Just another portfolio niche that should benefit from increased space capability demand.

Valuation

As for a valuation, Redwire shows us a bit of a mixed picture. I see both optimism from the market about future growth and also some scepticism about their current profitability. Since the company is not yet generating consistent earnings, it would be best to focus on revenue-based metrics. That being said, Redwire trades at about 3.3x trailing price-to-sales and about 5.6x EV to sales, both of which sit well above the sector medians. So clearly the market is assigning a premium to their top-line growth potential here, which would likely be driven by the number of emerging opportunities in space infrastructure, defence tech, and high-value contracts we’re seeing, like ESA’s quantum-secure satellite initiative. However, we also have an extremely elevated forward EV to EBITDA multiple to imply that profitability is still very much distant, and investors would mainly be valuing the company on future expectations rather than current cash generation.

Elsewhere, the stock trades at a pretty notable discount on a balance sheet basis. The price-to-book ratio sits around 1.6x to 1.7x, or about 48% to 50% below the sector median. Now, this tells me that the market is not fully valuing Redwire’s net assets here and would potentially reflect some concerns about execution risk and integration challenges from their roll-up strategy. Overall, investors would seem to be pricing in decent revenue growth and a very valuable positioning in next-generation space and defence markets, but they also seem to be discounting the company’s ability to convert that growth into durable profitability at the same time. So it’s a bit of a divergence, in which there’s a premium on sales multiples but discounted book value. What I'm trying to say here is that Redwire is being treated less like a mature industrial player and more like a high-risk, high-upside technology platform still trying to prove out its business model.

Profitability risk

We have to touch on their financial profile a bit here. I see a pretty clear disconnect between revenue growth and underlying business quality. This would be particularly true when we look at their backlog coverage and margin performance. As you know, the company managed to exit the year with a backlog of about $400 million, with FY25 revenues hitting $335 million. This would suggest just over one year of revenue visibility, which is well below the typical 2 to 3 year coverage that I would like to see in an aerospace/defence name. So there’s limited backlog depth, which would propose that Redwire is pretty heavily dependent on short-cycle contracts.

They also seem to value continuous order inflow to sustain growth, which we can see in the 2026 revenue guidance of $450 to $500 million. Now, if they want to achieve that target, I think they’ll need a good deal of new bookings rather than simply executing on existing contracts. So this would introduce a higher degree of forecasting risk, and I think it’s safe to say we can price in some more stock volatility here compared to peers who have much longer-duration programs. Just on that stock volatility, in the past six months, we’ve seen shares as high as the mid-$14 range and also as low as the mid-$5 range. As for profitability, the trends have deteriorated somewhat despite decent top-line expansion. If we start with revenue, it’s increased by about 10%, but gross profit fell, and operating losses have widened pretty dramatically to about $229.6 million.

Looking ahead

So going forward, the upside case for Redwire would remain centred on their unique and diversified exposure. It spans across a decent number of high-growth space/defence verticals. We can also combine this with strong validation from NASA and the ESA. I think if we see the company translate this kind of positioning into improved execution and margin expansion, they should also stand to benefit disproportionately from the long-term buildout of space infrastructure. It makes it a high-upside, albeit execution-dependent, opportunity.

data.title: T-Mobile Is Now More Attractive Than Deutsche Telekom
data.description:
Fundamentals matter even for T-Mobile

Back in September 2025, I wrote that T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS) was less attractive than Deutsche Telekom AG (DTEGY, DTEGF). My readers know why: Although most of Deutsche Telekom’s value resides in its 53% T-Mobile stake, the two stocks follow very different trading dynamics—which creates nice investment opportunities every now and then.

Seven months ago, on a fundamental basis, T-Mobile was expensive and priced for perfection, making it vulnerable to any disappointment in future results, while Deutsche Telekom’s European business was simply too cheap, despite consistent growth.

Up until yesterday, this valuation discrepancy led to completely different performances of the two stocks: TMUS has lost 24%, while DTEGY was roughly flat (dividends included for both) compared to September 2025.

As a result, 53% of T-Mobile is now worth $114.3B, while Deutsche Telekom’s total market cap is $167B, leaving $52.7B for the European business. This means it trades for about 12.2x FCF (2026e), which is the highest valuation it has enjoyed for a long time. On the other hand, T-Mobile trades for just 11.4x FCF (2026e). It hasn’t traded for such a low valuation for many years. (You can find current consensus expectations in great detail on the Deutsche Telekom website.)

Given the better growth outlook for T-Mobile, I would expect the U.S. business to outperform the EU stub at this point, and at the very least it should definitely not underperform.

Interesting discrepancies

A quite funny discrepancy occurred in JPMorgan’s (JPM) coverage of the two names: A few weeks ago, the U.S. analyst highlighted the increased probability that T-Mobile might outperform too dire expectations for its Q1/26 results, while just yesterday the same firm's overseas analyst saw nothing but clouds on the horizon for the very same U.S. business his colleague is so bullish for and therefore downgraded not T-Mobile but Deutsche Telekom, which caused a rare 6% sell-off in Germany.

While we may debate the rationale behind the two different postures, fundamentals speak a very clear language: Based on FCF expectations, TMUS seems to be more attractive than Deutsche Telekom’s European business at this point.

Even with all possible clouds on the horizon, T-Mobile is still expected to grow FCF by some 30% over the next three years, while Deutsche Telekom’s European business should not grow more than half of that rate.

Sure, the competitive environment in the U.S. is changing, Verizon (VZ) seems to be willing to sacrifice some margin, and AT&T (T) has enjoyed some success with its recent initiatives—which means consensus expectations for TMUS might still come down a bit. Yet it remains hard to justify why Deutsche Telekom’s European hodgepodge of slow-growth businesses should trade at a higher valuation than the far easier-to-understand and very likely higher-growth business in the U.S.—especially since Deutsche Telekom itself doesn’t expect its ex-U.S. results to outperform:

Quite the contrary: Since so far the EU business has been underperforming compared to the communicated long-term outlook at the Capital Markets Day, while the overall growth outlook for the group has been maintained, the implication is that T-Mobile is outperforming and will continue to compensate for the shortfall from Europe. This becomes even more evident when considering the unfavorable forex development in the meantime, which has burdened U.S. results in Euro terms.

Other important details

Both companies are buying back shares. While Deutsche Telekom has a rather small program of just $2.3B (or a bit more than 1% of its market cap), T-Mobile returns a whopping $14B to its shareholders (representing 6.5% of its market cap). The cheaper the stock is, the more value is added through repurchases, which means that even from this perspective, TMUS is the more attractive stock.

However, since 53% of the value creation at T-Mobile flows through to Deutsche Telekom, the indirect accretion is very interesting even for DTEGY shareholders.

Secondly, depending on where investors are based, forex becomes an important consideration: The falling USD has already burdened results as reported in EUR by Deutsche Telekom, and T-Mobile’s lower market cap combined with the lower exchange rate has rapidly inverted the situation as highlighted just seven months ago.

If the USD moves further down from here, investors based in Europe may not benefit as much from a TMUS re-rating as U.S.-based investors.

How to play

The easiest way would be long TMUS, which is ok for U.S. investors but a bit more risky for forex-exposed European investors.

The smartest way would be long TMUS/short DTEGY, which would basically concentrate the bet on the valuation discrepancy. If I am right, TMUS should outperform DTEGY from here, and the arbitrage should pay off. – However, keep in mind that a falling USD could at least somewhat reduce your gains in this scenario, as it would lift the DTEGY part of the arbitrage.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fortive: Starting To Improve Its Performance
data.description:
Shares of Fortive Corporation (FTV) have recently come to life, this being a welcome sign after the business was separated into two separate companies last summer. The company separated its precision technologies business into Ralliant Corporation (RAL) to unlock value, but at the time, I ended up concluding that both shares were largely fairly valued.

Shares of Fortive have outperformed those of Ralliant. The latter stock is being hurt by overhanging corporate cost allocation, to the benefit of Fortive, it appears. The momentum in the business returning is welcomed, as the focus is on execution, and it seems that Fortive has rather quickly delivered on execution here.

This has driven a modest re-rating higher, for the right reasons, as shares look fairly valued with some potential for a further re-rating. That is, if organic growth can be maintained or extended here, it is a job to be demonstrated by the management team.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Ending Its First Year Alone

In February, Fortive reported its first full-year set of results on its own, that is, following the spin-off from Ralliant. Full-year sales rose by nearly 2% to $4.16 billion, with organic sales up by a similar two percent. However, reported growth accelerated to more than 4% in the fourth quarter, of which just over 3% was achieved on an organic basis.

Following the divestment of precision technologies through Ralliant, the company consists of two segments. Intelligent operation solutions grew reported sales by just over 2% to nearly $2.9 billion, making up nearly 70% of sales, with segment margins reported at around a quarter of sales. The segment includes professional instrumentation for maintenance, repair, condition monitoring, gas detection solutions, facilities, and asset lifecycle software, brought to the market through brands like Fluke.

Advanced Healthcare Solutions grew sales by just over a percent to $1.3 billion, with segment margins reported at just around 10% of sales. Note that these are GAAP operating profit margins, as EBITDA margins (with most of the reconciliation relating to amortization) are much higher. Activities include instrument sterilization, consumables, test equipment, and critical workflow software under the brand names of ASP and others.

After corporate cost allocation, a GAAP EBIT number of $720 million looks very solid, as net earnings of $579 million came in at $1.73 per share on a diluted basis. Adjusted earnings were reported at $2.71 per share, which looks quite clean if amortization charges are stripped out, up thirty cents on the year before. Net debt is reported at $2.8 billion, while adjusted EBITDA comes in at nearly $1.2 billion, for a leverage ratio approaching the mid-2s.

Supported by the solid momentum in the fourth quarter, the company guides for adjusted earnings to advance to $2.90-$3.00 per share in 2026, which looks quite solid. This is based on an expectation of 2-3% organic growth, some margin gains, and continued contribution from stock buybacks. Trading around the $57 mark in mid-February, that implies a 19-20 times earnings multiple, all of which looks quite fair, also provided the guidance provided.

Settling Down

The reality is that the performance described above is decent and not spectacular, but that is fine. Comforting is that earnings are seen a bit higher than I thought to be realistic last summer.

This comes as accurately predicting earnings post-separation is difficult amidst debt allocated across entities, as the same applies to corporate cost, with potential for synergies and dis-synergies considered as well. In fact, shares of Ralliant have been struggling, in part the result of corporate cost overhang, providing a benefit to Fortive here.

Part of the higher earnings numbers has come from solid execution and also fairly aggressive buybacks by Fortive last year, after $1.6 billion was spent on buybacks.

Note that Fortive has experience with spin-offs, as ahead of the Ralliant spin-off, the company spun out Vontier Corporation (VNT) in 2020; those shares have been trading stagnant ever since. The idea of these spin-offs is to create a smaller and more focused business, and while recently organic growth has improved a bit, the reality is that the company feels underleveraged to megatrends.

For one, the electrification/data center theme is not seen here, and while instrumentation, healthcare, and predictive maintenance all sound good, these feel like GDP+ or GDP-alike growth stories, but nothing too strong beyond that.

And Now?

With Fortive finding itself on a more solid footing, with growth accelerating, investors can look forward to modest growth and earnings growth as well, as the company has rather quickly found itself performing on a relatively stable footing. This resilient performance has made it so that shares have risen from the high forties in the summer to the high fifties, with the performance being solid.

This is supported by the stronger earnings growth, as valuation multiples remain very reasonable here. Trading around 20 times forward earnings, these earnings being realistic and leverage seen in the mid-2s, the valuation at large looks quite fair for a business growing organic sales by 2-3%.

While a continuation of growth and re-rating by the market might rather easily push up earnings multiples to 25 times, and shares might advance to $70-$75 in such a scenario, that seems a logical ceiling in the valuation for now. However, we are far from achieving that, as current economic conditions and developments across the globe might easily create a more sluggish growth path. That would limit or eliminate the potential for growth to accelerate and thereby limit valuation multiple expansion, laying out a case for flattish near-term returns.

Given all this, my regard for Fortive has appreciated a bit, and the situation is much clearer here. Now the very hard part comes with divestments: designed to create a better-positioned and focused business, which has to translate into stronger growth here.

data.title: Q2 2026 Outlook: The Reset Is Your Entry Point
data.description:
After Q1 volatility, AI monetization, credit repricing, and fiscal risks are shaping a more selective but opportunity-rich environment for investors.

After a volatile start to the year, several areas of the market have reset, creating more attractive opportunities in Q2. While macro conditions remain broadly supportive, the opportunity set is increasingly driven by selectivity rather than broad exposure.

AI Compute and Semiconductor Demand Still Underestimated

The most important development this quarter is a shift in how AI is being monetized.

While early adoption was driven by consumers, the data now clearly shows that corporate America is opening its wallet. Companies are increasingly willing to invest heavily in AI due to the productivity gains it delivers, often completing tasks in minutes that previously took days.

We are seeing this firsthand at VanEck. Our own token usage has scaled to billions per day following enterprise rollouts of ChatGPT and Claude, offering a real-world example of how quickly these tools are being embedded into workflows.

This combination of rising adoption alongside improving efficiency helps explain why total AI spending continues to grow. Even as costs per unit fall, the number of use cases and users is expanding rapidly.

Despite this, markets remain skeptical that current levels of capital expenditure can continue over the longer term. We believe that skepticism may be misplaced. Companies are unlikely to cut spending on a technology that delivers clear efficiency gains.

As a result, semiconductors remain a high-conviction area, with markets potentially underestimating the durability of AI-driven demand

Private Credit: Fear vs. Reality

Private credit continues to dominate headlines, but the underlying data tells a more measured story.

Default rates remain relatively low, and the broader U.S. economy remains resilient. Yet many business development companies (BDCs) are trading at discounts that imply significantly higher levels of stress than current fundamentals would suggest.

This disconnect creates opportunity. Publicly traded BDCs benefit from structural advantages, such as the absence of forced selling, which make them more resilient than many investors assume.

At the same time, the valuation reset has extended to the managers themselves. Firms such as Ares (ARES) and Blue Owl (OBDC), which previously traded at elevated multiples, now appear far more reasonably valued relative to their long-term earnings potential.

Hedge Fiscal Uncertainty with Gold

While the fiscal outlook had been improving, new spending proposals have reintroduced uncertainty. Higher deficits could place upward pressure on long-term interest rates, which would have broad implications for financial markets. This is one of the key risks we are monitoring closely.

Gold remains a critical hedge in this environment. Importantly, its long-term drivers extend beyond inflation or short-term geopolitical events. Instead, gold is supported by global wealth growth and a gradual shift away from reliance on the U.S. dollar.

Short-term volatility, such as recent selling tied to energy price shocks, does not change this longer-term trend. We continue to view gold as a strategic allocation, particularly on pullbacks.

More broadly, commodity markets are also being shaped by geopolitical developments. In the near term, shifts in the futures curve into backwardation can create additional return drivers for investors, particularly through positive roll yield. However, these dynamics are cyclical and should be understood as part of a broader allocation strategy.

India Remains Long-Term Opportunity Despite Volatility

India continues to offer compelling long-term growth potential, even after a period of underperformance. Structural reforms, favorable demographics, and rapid digital adoption remain intact. At the same time, the market is undergoing a period of adjustment, with traditional business models being disrupted and valuations becoming more attractive.

For long-term investors, this combination of structural growth and improved entry points remains compelling.

Bitcoin Enters a More Complex Cycle

Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) traditional four-year cycle appears to have shifted, creating a more nuanced near-term outlook. While past cycles were marked by sharp drawdowns following halving events, recent price behavior suggests a more range-bound environment. This may reflect broader institutional participation and lower volatility.

While the near-term outlook is mixed, the long-term case for adoption remains intact. Current levels may represent a more constructive entry point for investors with a longer horizon.

Where to Allocate Now

Following recent volatility, the investment landscape is offering more targeted opportunities, particularly in areas where fundamentals remain intact despite sentiment weakening and valuations pulling back.

For investors, Q2 is less about broad market direction and more about identifying where resets have created more attractive entry points.

  • Lean into AI and semiconductors as enterprise demand continues to drive sustained growth.
  • Consider adding exposure to BDCs and private credit managers after recent valuation resets.
  • Maintain gold as a portfolio hedge against rising fiscal risks and higher long-term rates.
  • Look to India for long-term growth as structural trends remain intact despite volatility.
  • Approach Bitcoin opportunistically at current levels with a long-term perspective.

Important Disclosures

Bitcoin (BTC) is a decentralized digital currency, without a central bank or single administrator, that can be sent from user to user on the peer-to-peer Bitcoin network without the need for intermediaries.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. data.title: Greenlight Capital Q4 2025 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partner:

The Greenlight Capital funds (the “Partnerships”) returned 9.0% in 2025, net of fees and expenses, compared to 17.9% for the S&P 500 index. In the fourth quarter, the Partnerships returned 8.5%, while the S&P 500 index returned 2.7%. Since the inception of Greenlight Capital in May 1996, the Partnerships have returned 3,406% cumulatively, or 12.7% annualized, both net of fees and expenses. Over the same period, the S&P 500 index has returned 1,693% or 10.2% annualized. Greenlight’s investors have earned $6.1 billion, net of fees and expenses, since inception.1

Many have reached out to express concern over New York City’s new mayor. We confess to sharing some of that apprehension. After all, we are not Democratic Socialists. However, when we hear President Trump say things like, “I, as President of the United States, am calling for a one-year cap on Credit Card Interest Rates of 10%,” and “We [the government] should take stakes in companies when people need something,” perhaps concerns about socialism should extend beyond the five boroughs. We wonder if in a few years there will be a trivia game called, “Who said it: Trump or Mamdani?”

As we reflect on these uncertainties and think about our investment strategy and portfolio construction, our goal is to build a bottom-up portfolio of equity longs that are absolutely cheap and, where possible, misunderstood, paired with a portfolio of equity shorts that are overvalued, poorly understood and deteriorating. We complement this with a macro book, partly designed to hedge macro risks and partly to capture macro insights. We may also use index positions to adjust overall exposure to align with our top-down thinking. In a typical year, we would expect most of our performance to come from the long-short book, with a small contribution from macro.

For 2025, we made all our return and alpha in the macro portfolio, as shown in the table below:

Gross Contribution Net Contribution Alpha2
Longs 3.6% 2.4% -8.8%
Shorts -6.4% -6.3% 9.3%
Shorts (index) 0.2% 0.1% 0.0%
Macro 14.4% 12.8% 13.9%
Total 11.8% 9.0% 14.5%

All told, by our calculation, we generated 14.5% of alpha, which on its own is a perfectly acceptable result. Macro investing continued to shine, even as the long-short portfolio lagged.

When we have a macro insight, the goal is to express it as directly as possible, such that we are most likely to succeed or fail based on the insight itself. For example, if we are bullish on gold, copper or interest rates, we should own gold, copper or interest rates. We judge our strategy in this regard to have been effective. Others might express a similar view by being long equities of mining companies or interest rate-sensitive financial institutions. If we try to express our view on copper by owning equity in a copper mining company, we are also exposed to what happens at the mines or to hedging decisions made by the company.

On the other hand, we are neither bullish nor bearish on coal prices. We own Core Natural Resources (CNR) because we believe that coal stocks in general are hated, and we believe we own a superior asset, at a very attractive valuation, run by capable operators. Of course, we are subject to commodity price fluctuations and mine operations, neither of which went our way this year. Coal prices fell, and one of CNR's mines had a fire.

It was a challenging year in the long-short portfolio. In general, the economy was quite weak in the sectors in which we were long. We own mostly established businesses that provide goods or services and are therefore subject to macro risk. We can contrast this with companies that hope to provide goods or services in the sometimes-distant future, where investors are less concerned with current business results, making a slower economy less impactful on the narrative.

From a pure macro perspective, it was a tough year for homebuilding, chemicals, packaging, coal, oil services, agriculture and personal computers. A good chunk of the economy is experiencing a cyclical downturn (some might even say a recession). A large percentage of our longs in these sectors earned less in 2025 than we anticipated at the beginning of the year. Many of the disappointments stemmed from circumstances beyond management's control and do not reflect a deterioration in the long-term outlook for these businesses.

Typically, in cyclical businesses, when earnings fall short for cyclical reasons, the P/E multiples expand (unless the deterioration creates a risk of insolvency). The idea is that the market should be willing to pay a higher multiple on trough earnings. Unfortunately, this has not happened to most of our companies. The earnings fell short, and the multiples held steady or even contracted a bit.

We did have a few notable successes in the long portfolio this year. The highlights were:

  • Brighthouse Financial (BHF): After years of frustration, the company put itself up for sale, which led to a sale process that culminated in a buyer agreeing to purchase the company. While the valuation at less than two-thirds of book value is not exciting, it does provide us with a reasonable exit from a challenged situation. We likely will write more about this after we do, in fact, exit.
  • The Fluor/NuScale Power stub (FLR/SMR): FLR owned the majority of SMR, which essentially went parabolic. We anticipated FLR would dispose of its SMR stake and use the proceeds to repurchase its own shares, and this is exactly what is happening. The result of the heavy sales by FLR combined with SMR's very speculative valuation has led to a sharp decline in SMR, while FLR has been largely stable. The result is that the value of our stub position increased substantially.
  • Victoria's Secret (VSCO): A prior management team nearly destroyed one of the most well-known brands in the world. To be more politically correct, it got rid of sexy and eliminated its world-famous fashion show. New management is reversing those decisions and the brand is beginning to recover. The stock, which was all but left for dead, is beginning to reflect the brand's recovery.
  • Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA): The company continued to deliver rapid growth in its patented branded portfolio, with results consistently exceeding expectations. The market began to reflect this progress only after policy headwinds eased, including a negotiated pricing agreement with the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services for TEVA's drug Austedo beginning in 2027. These developments more than offset muted performance in the generics business and highlight the progress TEVA has made in its transition to a branded pharmaceutical company.

Many of the remaining large names in our long portfolio suffered for the reasons described above, though thankfully we didn't suffer any major meltdowns. The closest would be Lanxess (Germany: LXS)(LNXSF), which suffered a cyclical decline and a loss of market share to China in one of its three segments. We believe that other cyclical names that hurt us, including CNH Industrial (CNH), CNR, Graphic Packaging (GPK), Solvay (Belgium: SOLB)(SVYSF) and United Parks & Resorts (PRKS) will likely recover in due course. Green Brick Partners (GRBK) also faced strong cyclical headwinds, but the stock still managed to gain 11%, while most other homebuilders were flat or down.

The short portfolio performed the opposite of the long portfolio. Most of the companies faced difficult economic conditions and underperformed the market or declined outright. Our shorts of high-multiple consumer companies with primarily Democratic customers did well. Various homebuilder and housing-related shorts also performed well. One short that bedeviled us in 2024 suffered a sharp reversal in early 2025, allowing us to recover a chunk of our prior losses. The SMR short component of the FLR/SMR stub trade contributed positively. Finally, the double-levered MicroStrategy ETFs (MSTU and MSTX) fell about 90%.

Nonetheless, the short portfolio lost money in 2025. While it is difficult to fully explain the result without disclosing names, we can offer the following observations:

We suffered from one short that did not have remarkable overall stock performance (the operating results were far below beginning-of-year expectations) but had a lot of volatility, which we traded poorly. A second short, which had been successful for several years and appeared to be on the path to bankruptcy, instead became a meme stock, resulting in the loss of our previous profits and then some. A third short is a money-losing insurance company that has attracted a cultish following. A fourth short is a money-losing medical devices company that will likely face competition and market saturation before it turns an acceptable profit but achieved revenue growth in 2025 that was a few percent better than expected. Finally, we had one AI-centered short that went against us. Ultimately, we expect this company to fail. Fortunately, the position was of appropriate small size at inception. Our overall exposure to AI remains quite small, though we did initiate a new medium-sized short in the fourth quarter.

Turning to macro, nearly all our 2025 investments were successful. Obviously, gold was the highlight. Gold had an exceptional year and appreciated 64%. While our core thesis on gold is that imprudent fiscal and monetary policies in the world's largest countries will eventually cause a significant problem, at which point it will make sense to hold a lot of gold, we do not believe that was the primary driver this year. Instead, as the U.S. is losing its hegemonic leadership and has demonstrated serious risk to counterparties through, among other things, the freezing of Russian reserve assets and unstable tariff policy, other countries are seeking to reduce their exposure to dollars for reserves and for trade. The result is that many foreign central banks are increasing gold reserves and establishing it as an alternative to settling global trade in dollars.

Notably, it appears that gold is being re-monetized within official government channels. This, of course, is what the bitcoin crowd thought would happen for bitcoin (we still aren't sure what the Fartcoin people thought might happen). Compared to a year ago at least, it has become more likely that gold will remain gold and bitcoin will fail to take over as digital gold.

Copper was another notable winner, as our medium-sized long position, expressed via options on copper futures, performed very well. Copper prices rose about 40% in 2025, as the AI world needs more copper and supply remains limited. Further, several large mines around the world experienced production shortfalls.

We also had a notable gain in interest rate futures, where we correctly anticipated that the U.S. Federal Reserve would cut rates further than the market expected. We had several other smaller gains in the macro book (including sovereign debt and foreign currency) and a small loss on inflation swaps, as inflation expectations declined modestly and realized inflation came in around market expectations.

From a top-down perspective, we continue to be concerned about market valuations. We believe that the U.S. equity market is the most expensive we've seen since we began managing money, and arguably in the history of the United States. It isn't just our skepticism about AI stocks; speculative behavior among retail investors is palpable. Many others have gone to great lengths to explain the market valuation issue. Their work is easy to find, and we won't try to replicate it here.

From a long-term perspective, we still believe that this is not a great time to have a lot of equity exposure. Admittedly, this has been our view for the last couple of years, and yet the market has continued to advance. In 2025, our daily correlation to the market was -0.11.

Our top-down caution has prevented us from participating in the Beta that the market has offered. However, we see many things that could go wrong and believe our cautious behavior will ultimately be rewarded by having cash on hand to invest in an improved opportunity set. In the meantime, our goal is to achieve a reasonable result, while not getting run over by being too bearish. On this basis, we judge 2025 to be an adequate, if uninspiring, result.

We initiated several small long positions during the quarter.

Antero Resources (AR) is a natural gas exploration and production company with operations in the Marcellus Shale in West Virginia. The company operates at the low end of the cost curve and has a stable production profile with significant acreage. In December, AR announced an acquisition that we believe will be accretive to earnings and will extend the runway of its future production. We acquired our shares at an average price of $33.92, implying an 11% free cash flow yield. AR shares ended the quarter at $34.46.

Deckers Outdoor (DECK) designs, markets and distributes footwear and apparel, including the Hoka (running shoes) and UGG (casual luxury cold-weather shoes) brands. DECK stock declined after it posted disappointing results early in 2025, which were impacted by tariff uncertainty, a warehouse transition in Europe, and warm weather during the early part of the UGG selling season. As Hoka expands internationally, we expect growth to re-accelerate and believe the company will use its strong balance sheet, including net cash equal to roughly 9% of its market capitalization, to increase the pace of stock repurchases. We acquired our shares at an average price of $85.49. DECK ended the quarter at $103.67.

Global Payments (GPN) is a payments processor serving mostly small and mid-size merchants in the U.S. After exiting the position in 2023 at $108.61, we re-acquired shares at an average price of $77.85. In 2024, the company surprised investors by announcing a two-step transaction to sell its issuer processing business and acquire Worldpay. While the market did not like the announcement, we understand the logic behind it and the likely synergies it should create. Pro forma, GPN is targeting $5 billion of free cash flow in 2028, which equates to nearly 25% of the current pro forma combined market capitalization. Once the Worldpay transaction closes, we expect GPN's consistent history of organic growth and its commitment to return nearly $7 billion to shareholders (one-third of the market cap) over the next two years to be better appreciated by the market and to provide runway for the shares to re-rate higher. GPN ended the quarter at $77.40.

Henry Schein (HSIC) is a leading distributor of dental and medical products. Since COVID, cost inefficiencies have led to margin erosion that we believe is now set to be addressed by newly installed activist board members and a pending CEO change. Should the company be successful in correcting these issues and improving its e-commerce capabilities, we expect margin improvement that will lead to significant free cash flow generation and share buybacks. We purchased our shares at an average price of $68.11. HSIC shares ended the quarter at $75.58.

Spectrum Brands Holdings (SPB) is a consumer products company focused on pet care, home & garden, and home & personal care (HPC). The company faced challenges in 2025 that now appear largely behind it, and we expect SPB to return to growth this year. There is also potential for the company to realize proceeds from a divestiture of its HPC segment, which we believe the market currently values at zero. In the meantime, the company is returning capital through sizeable share repurchases. We acquired our shares at an average price of $52.75, and SPB ended the quarter at $59.08.

Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) is a media conglomerate that owns HBO, the Warner Bros. Studio and content library, and various linear cable networks. We acquired our shares at an average price of $22.66 after Paramount Skydance (PSKY) bid $23.50 for the company, as we expected additional bidders to emerge. Shortly thereafter, Netflix submitted a $27.75 bid for WBD's streaming and studio assets, prompting PSKY to raise its offer to $30 per share. We believe a sale in the low- to mid-$30s is the most likely outcome. WBD shares ended the quarter at $28.82.

We exited HP Inc. (HPQ) with a tiny gain and Seadrill (SDRL) with a tiny loss. In both instances, our initial thesis was correct and we had early gains, but we overstayed our welcome.

In HPQ's case, we benefited from the Windows refresh cycle. However, AI computers have not materialized into a notable upswing in business. Prospectively, the company faces a new challenge from soaring memory prices that are likely to force substantial increases in PC prices and could have a large negative impact on volumes and/or margins. We therefore decided to exit.

In SDRL's case, our thesis rested on rising day rates for drilling rigs. We missed the inflection and wound up with an unremarkable outcome.

At the end of the quarter, Gav Balasingam departed Greenlight and will be taking some time off before beginning his next adventure. Best of luck, Gav!

After our first few years building Greenlight, we closed the funds with limited exceptions between 2000 and 2019. Even during our difficult period from 2015 to 2018, when we had poor performance and experienced substantial redemptions, we remained closed. It has never been our goal to manage a large amount of assets.

However, in May 2020, we expressed our belief that it was a good time to invest in Greenlight and decided to begin repopulating our investor base. Since then, the Partnerships have generated annualized performance of 21%, compared to 18% for the S&P 500, with a daily correlation to the market of 0.12 and no down years.

We've had fundraising success and rebuilt our asset base with a large and diverse group of investors. We are again at a size that supports the business and have decided to close the Partnerships on July 1. While we don't know what the future will bring, our base expectation is that we will remain closed, perhaps indefinitely. Nonetheless, as we've met with many current and prospective investors, we promised that we would give proper notice before closing, allowing time to complete diligence and reach a final decision. Consider this letter to be that notice. If you are interested in investing, please indicate that to us no later than May 1. It is possible that if there is substantial interest by this deadline, we may ask that new investments be staged to minimize portfolio disruption. If you'd like to discuss this in more detail, please contact Jason.

At year-end, the largest disclosed long positions in the Partnerships were Brighthouse Financial, Core Natural Resources, Fluor, Green Brick Partners and Solvay. The Partnerships had an average exposure of 89% long and 50% short.

"You measure the size of the accomplishment by the obstacles you had to overcome to reach your goals." – Booker T. Washington

Best Regards,

Greenlight Capital
